Event Control
Knowledge to Action
• Methods to increase awareness of system events for better system control
• Cyber Security Application
2
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Given the Nature of These Problems, What To Do?
• We’re a big corporation, unnecessary complexity is our thing!
• We can never get an accurate, up to date inventory so we can’t know exactly
what’s on our network
• We can’t see all of the traffic coming in and out of the network, and even if we
could, we’d never be able to look at it all.
• We spend all our time chasing and doing paperwork for false positive alerts
• The threats we need to watch change every day
3
Duh. But How? We’ve got problems:
• Advice from a simple systems sysadmin:
‣ Avoid unnecessary complexity
‣ Know what's happening on your systems
‣ Take care of the obvious problems automatically
‣ Check out and note anything weird
‣ Quickly
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Security Log Monitoring Platform as a Framework for New
Tools
• For simple systems that can be audited with high accuracy you can have fairly good control by:
keeping your inventory accurate, reading your logs, staying on top of anomalies, baselining all
known processes… But for complex networks where we cannot hope to accurately know these
things, we need some new tools to try to guess what’s happening.
• The Security Log Monitoring Platform (SLM) was designed and built to be capable of low cost,
high speed ingestion and analysis of system generated events
• SLM was built with a modular, expandable architecture to enable a plug and play approach to
support variable work loads as well as new and evolving analysis methods
• This modular approach allows for low cost, ala carte functionality as demonstrated in our recent
SLM2 release, however it also allows for implementation of high-end, advanced functionality
without requiring the system to be re-architected
• From the point of view of the original intent, we have delivered on part 1 of SLM (infrastructure
and basic log monitoring and correlation) and are now pushing into part 2 which is the design and
implementation of new advanced analysis tools for the platform.
4
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Understand Normal through True Positives and True
Negatives
• On simple networks with defined nodes and complete knowledge of activity, you can define normal
events (true negatives) and define explicit rules to detect malicious events (true positives) and
investigate what remains.
• As you discover and verify new normal behavior you add it to the true negatives list, as new identifiable
threats emerge you add them to the true positives list. Over time the remainder of unknown events to
investigate becomes manageable.
• For complex, poorly understood networks you still apply true positives, though engineered for scale and
applied only to the nodes and networks you’re aware of; but you still need to discover what is normal…
you don’t just know what normal is, leading to the high false positive rates that often turn million dollar
SIEM infrastructure into exercises in futility.
5
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Example Using Conditional Probability to Predict False
Positive Rates
• Here’s an example of applying Bayes Theorem of Conditional Probability to expose the Base Rate
Logical Fallacy that clouds our thinking about SIEMs (and leads to such a confusing marketplace):
• To use this example we plug in some values and make one assumption:
‣ Our SIEM tool is 98% effective. It can identify 98% of all the malware it sees
‣ We’ll use 1 million events as our sample size
‣ We assume 1 in 1000 events might be related to bad traffic
6
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Bayes Theorem
• Formally:
• In English: The probability of an event (A) given another event (B) equals the
probability of event (B) given event (A) times the probability of event (A), all
divided by the probability of event (B)
• In our terms: (A = Actual threat), (B= warning “Bell”), so… the chance of an
actual threat when there’s a warning bell is the same as the chance of an
warning bell when there is an actual threat times the chance of an actual threat,
all divided by the chance of a warning bell.
• When you run “Big Data” numbers though this formula, you get the counter-
intuitive results that demonstrate just how ineffective True-Positive only based
SIEMs can be.
See “Bayes tool for Siems.xlsx” for the worksheet to play with the numbers
7

Artificial Intelligence
• Often used interchangeably with the term “machine learning”, however machine learning is just one of
5 areas comprising the field of AI*
‣ Computer vision
‣ Speech
‣ Machine Learning
‣ Natural Language Processing
‣ Knowledge Graphs
* Andrew Ng, AI thought leader, Chief Scientist Baidu, Cofounder Google Brain, Stanford professor, the guy you quote in
PowerPoint slides about AI.
